transcript.transcript: February 12, 2007, and we're at the Idaho Historical Museum. And this is an interview with Tamalla Hart of Boise, Idaho. Tamalla, we're interested in knowing about, you've lived here for a long time, about some of your earliest memories of Gloria Davis Park. Okay, I'm a native of Boise. I've lived here all my life. My family's from here. When I was young, I used to... You were born in? 1958. And I learned how to ice skate on Julia Davis Park Pond. My cousin taught me how to jet. My sister and I used to go down there with her all the time and go skating. Uh-huh. And then... And who taught you to skate? Linda Lester. Her family has been skating all their lives. Was she a teacher? She's a skating teacher now, yes. Her parents were dance partners. Uh-huh. So it was a business? I think it was more recreational, I believe. I mean, but you paid her to teach you? Oh, she paid all the time. Oh, OK. And then our family used to have picnics in the park for family reunions all the time, the Lester's and the Sealips. And I don't remember what else we did. We used to come down and listen to the bands, the band Shell. Tell me about the bands. Do you remember what days those were? I don't remember those days. I just remember coming down and listening to them. I was also one of the first years that they did the walk for the cancer walk. We came down here and that's where they had their Indian celebration. It was down here in Julia Davis. Her walk for cancer? Yes, uh-huh. And then there was an Indian celebration? No, just at the Indian celebration. Oh, the Indian, sorry. That's okay. We used to play on the slides, the tall, tall slides that they had down there, and they had swings. Oh, I know one other thing too. My cousin came from Germany. He's a famous producer over in Germany. So, who is your cousin? His name is Hannah Hoot. How do you spell that? H-A-N-N-O-H-O-T-H And he's a famous film producer. Film producer from Germany? Yes. When he was young, he came over to visit us. And we've been riding back and forth. And he came to visit us. And we brought him down here to the zoo. And we went through the snake part of it. And I scared him really bad. Jerry and I also came down here in the summertime and sat by the lake and talked and watched all the people and the animals. And Jerry's your boyfriend? Yes, he is. And we used to come down when I was a kid too to go to the carnival, to play at the carnival. At the Fun Spot? Right, Fun Spot, yes. And then, What kind of rides were there? There was the merry-go-round. There was a Ferris wheel. There was a roller coaster. It was a roller coaster? Yes. There was... There was no... A roller coaster? No. And a parasail. A parasail, oh. There was a few other little kind of rides, but they were just like, you know, elephants or something. My kids and I even went on the paddle boats down there. And they had boats down there too, besides paddle boats. Oh really? What kind of boats were those? They were just like canoes. And then, Just before my son left for Iraq, we came down and had lunch and sat in the park and talked. Who's he back? He is just in North Carolina for rest right now. He's doing his two week leave. And he'll be back, tied back here shortly. It's his second tour. Is he in the Guard or the? He's in the Army. In the Army. Is he from here? He is from here, yes. I was trying to remember if we ever did anything for band here, and I don't recall any band. I could stop here and have hot dogs afterwards. I don't remember doing any of that stuff here. I think that was Ann Morrison Park. I think that's about all I can think of. Did you go to Art in the Park? I've been there several times, yes. And the park must be important to you because you responded to a... Something that was in the newspaper about the memories. Yes. Tell me a little bit about... I mean, this park must be pretty... It was a beautiful park and you could come down here and enjoy all the... I mean, there were no crowds down here because there was so much room. And you could come down here and enjoy all the, I mean, there were no crowds down here because there was so much room. And you could come down here and the trees were down here. And it was shade of picnic areas. It was just open space. And there was a lot of people down here all the time. And you could have your spot. And the other parks are crowded all the time. Oh, the other parks are crowded. Uh-huh. And of course, when I was a kid, too, we used to come to the museum all the time, too, for school. Uh-huh. What was the museum like then? Small and, I remember, it smelled musty. Ha ha ha. So you came here for field trips? Right. Did they have the two-headed cow? Yes, they did. I remember that, yes. I heard they were known as that. So you come here on buses? Yes, buses. I think we practically spent the day. Of course, I'm sure all of them do now still, too. Uh-huh. It was really interesting and a lot of fun. I remember that. I remember, too, when you could go through the whole park and drive through the whole park down the center of the trees, and you can't do that anywhere either. Uh-huh. So you could go down that lane. Uh-huh. Actually, even where they've got the one-way, it's a one-way road, you still have to go a few ways over to that. So you could go from Capitol to the zoo. Uh-huh. Tell me a little bit more about the ice skating. The whole pond would freeze over. You could spend all day, days and days and days, and skate on it because it was frozen over for weeks at a time. And it doesn't do that anymore. And was it a popular place? After a while, it became pretty popular. It was long before they had the skating ice greens in town. After we started doing it, other people started to do it. It was a lot of fun. How was it that your family started? Did you have a? My cousin just started coming down here, and we just started skating. So it was your cousin? Mm-hmm. And somebody told me that they used to have a fireplace at night. Was there a fireplace down there? I thought it was one by the lake. Yeah, there was one down there. I thought it was still there. Oh, OK. Maybe. We never used it here. You didn't use it? I had heard that some people at night would use them down here. Yeah, we never did. You did, you day skated down here. Yeah, day skated, yeah. When you were small and came down here, was the pond quite deep? I don't know, you know, I really don't know. You never took a dip? No. Okay. I have no idea. In fact, I never even saw it dry, so I wouldn't know. Uh-huh. Okay. Did you always feel safe in the park? I always felt safe, yeah. We never had a question about that. Uh-huh. In fact, I still feel safe in this particular park. I don't know why. I just do. I never thought about that. Well, and you came to the Women's Challenge once? Yes, it was one of the first ones that they had. And we spent a lot of time down here celebrating afterwards. And they had the end it here. How about the zoo? The zoo, so many times I've watched it change over the years. I think it's got a lot better than it used to be. The animals used to be harsh and they used to, like the deer and the elk used to be, look really awful all the time. And I just never, I never used to joke about that. You didn't like to enjoy it? Because there were just not enough animals to be entertaining. Do you like it now? Yeah, it's a lot better now, yes. How about the Rose Garden? I actually have a friend that has a plaque over there. Her name is Marsha. That's really strappy. They have a memorial plaque over there for Malvinson's. Oh, a memorial plaque? Mm-hmm. And that's been there forever. We used to have our picnic sometimes by there, too. We'd go through there afterwards. Have you ever come to a wedding or an event like that? Not. It's the only place I haven't been for a wedding. How about the tour train? Actually, yes. I've ridden the tour train a couple of times. Actually, my sons used to help with the tour trains with Sun Valley Stages. They used to take them and do whatever to fix them, clean them, whatever. They used to work for Sun Valley Stages, so they used to work with them a lot. Oh, Sun Valley Stage is where you were. That was before they sold them. Uh-huh. Your son's here. Do you have two boys? I have two boys, Adrian and Dan. Uh-huh. And you had, were they big reunions that you had with your family? The whole entire family would come on both sides. We'd have two separate ones, but of course, because we were two different families. But yeah, everybody would come down here because there was lots of room. And we loved to come down here. There was stuff for everybody to do, you know. And there were enough picnic tables for everybody? Yeah, we got stuff. You brought stuff? Yeah, there was plenty of room for everybody, and we still had more room and we played you know, frisbee or whatever. Did you go to the Pete Cedar concert when it was here? Yeah. Were you, I see that was in the 60s, you're too young. Yeah, probably. Yeah, okay. Yeah, you were just so a kid. Yeah. Yeah, because that was a big con. Was it? Very controversial. I was rather offended when my sons and I were over here by the train, we were over here looking at the train one time, and that was when they had one of the first gay parades over here. I was really offended. You were offended at the gay parade? Yes, because I had no warning that they were going to be here, and, you know, I didn't want my kids to see something like that. Uh-huh. Because we were over there looking at the train. They were just, you know, looking through the park. Walking over the mires or something? Yeah. Oh, uh-huh. About what year was that? I've actually killed years ago. Probably 19... 90, 95 maybe? 98, 95? Yeah. Okay. I always like to come down with a big mic and family's having a cool seat. Uh-huh, that's where you were when you were a child. Yeah. My son has had lots of Volkswagen shows here. His group comes here all the time to have shows. He had once a Volkswagen car. And where did they have their cars? All over the park. All over the park. So is it more on the end where the IMK, down at the east end? Yeah. So they just, where they park their cars just on the? They just come park and they have picnics and have shows. Is that like every year? Yes. They said they have their October Fest. Oh, OK. And that's for just Volkswagen. Are there some for other kinds of cars, too? That's the one I know of. I don't know. OK. Uh-huh. And do you know that they have to make special arrangements to do it? I'm sure they do, but he didn't tell me, so I don't know. Uh-huh. Yeah. Yeah. So, it sounds like everybody in your family has... Did your parents use... My dad said he used to live over here where Big Mike is. There was a little house over there on the corner. He said he used to live over there on that corner. Your father lived on the corner where Big Mike is? He said he doesn't remember coming over here and doing anything but the picnics and stuff. So there were houses. It wasn't a park then. No, no. It was over by the big back. There were some houses over by there on the end. Oh, yeah. Right. I know where you're at. I should come here. Yeah. OK. So your father lived over there. Lived over there. So was that when he was a little boy? It was when he was a little boy, but he didn't tell me the date or time. He lived all over town so I could... Oh, he lived in various houses? Yeah. So what he talked to you about was just the picnics and that? Uh-huh. Uh-huh. My mother's deceased so she couldn't tell me anything. Uh-huh. How about fishing? Did anybody in your family fish? They did, but not over here. I remember that. I used to do that when I was a kid, though. I don't... I wouldn't want to fish over there. One of the kids there, there used to be people fish over there. I remember that. I forgot about that. Where would they fish over by? At the pond. Oh, in the pond. Oh, not in the river. Well, that too. I've seen a lot of people fish. We've all fished in the neighborhood. But this was the pond. Uh-huh. Oh, OK. Did you see people catch shark? Yeah. In fact, they still do today. Is that right? Uh-huh. I forgot all about that. Yeah, lots of things. Are there any other kinds of, sounds like you have a lot of different kinds of activities. I mean with your boys doing the shows, And you're doing like walks for cancer and fun runs and things like that? Of course, we've got a green belt like everybody else. Where do you usually start out on the green belt? Ammerson Park. At Ammerson, uh-huh. Yeah, Jerry and I walked seven miles one time on it. Wow. So you've used it for a long time. Uh-huh. So, well, and you went to Franklin Elementary? I did. And that's, they had, they would bring you down here on bus. Uh-huh. Wow. I could go to the library and then I'd go to the museum, I think, that we used to do. Uh-huh. How about the art museum? Have you been inside of that? Oh, I've never been in there. You've Never been in there? Never been in there, no. Who's in charge of admission? I don't know why they always issue. I don't know. It's just the only place I've been. I don't know why. How about the Black History Museum? I have not been there. My mother was going to try to donate some artifacts that they had there. I don't know why she didn't. Because we're related. Goblethangle is what came with our family across from England, through Africa. What was that? Gobo Fingo came from... Who is that a person? That's his name. What's his name? Gobo Fingo. Go... G-O-B-O. And Fingo, F-A-N-G-O. And that's a person? Yeah. He was a slave and they smuggled him from England through Africa to here. To Boise? Mm-hmm. They have all the history and everything on it. You do? So he was smuggled from England? Through Africa, yeah. So he was in England, And then he went to Africa. And about what year was this? 18-something. I don't remember. And how was it that you're to Boise? And how was it that your mother? It's her side of the family that smuggled in through. And your mother is a student? Lester. How do you spell? Lester, L-E-S-T-E-R. Lester, yeah. Lester. And with the Lester family in Boise, who helped get him here? And what was... How did they... They came on a ship and they smuggled him on the ship. They rolled him up underneath dresses and then carpets and... I've never heard that story. I've sent it to you. They rolled him up in carpets on a ship. And the ship was in the 1800s so slavery was illegal then. It was after the Civil War. Well, Boise, There weren't that many people in Boise in the 18... Well, it was just a territory in the 1860s, during the Civil War. So you think it was probably... How long has your family been here? Well, we've been here... Let's see, my dad's family's been here since 18... Early... The last... A lot of... A lot of part of 1800s. My mom's family's been here... Oh my gosh. Okay. I don't know how far back they lived here, because they came from England to here, so I don't remember how far. So, it's your... Do you remember why your great-great-whatever... I'll have to read it. I don't remember why they were writing it. There's something they've written about it? Is it a biography? Or is it a published book? No, I don't think it's published books. The newspapers have articles on it, and I have several pages of stuff on it. Is that something that you'd be willing to let us look at and copy? Sure. OK. Is it something that would be of value to have as a part of Idaho's history? Yes. And it was written, do you know who wrote it? I don't know if I have to look. I don't know. Because I've not heard of this person. But your mother went to the Black History Museum to see if they would take it. I don't remember why. You don't know whether they took it or not? No, they didn't. I just don't remember why. Oh, maybe she didn't have enough. I don't know. She might have been willing to share whatever I don't know. OK. Well, I'd like to follow that up on that with you. OK. Because that goes way back. Mm-hmm. And it's interesting. OK. Anything else about what your parents remember about the early days when they were children. Anything you can talk to me about? Not too much about this park, no. Not about this park. I can tell you more about Amherst. You know more about Amherst. It was the new park when you were young. Yeah, because they had the fountain. And they used to change colors. And we did lots of things on the river. It was closer to where we lived. Did you live on the bench? Yes. Uh-huh. When we took my cousin, one of the famous film producers, we took him to foot in the river. And we got out over there and had a picnic. So how did he become a famous film producer? You know, I don't know. Because we've kind of lost touch since he came from left here. His mom came here. And then after that, we just haven't been able to be in touch or anything. Oh, his mother was from Boise. No, no. They were originally all from Germany. And they had four brothers. The four brothers split and went to different countries. And She was a nurse during the war. She ended up in Boise? No, they just corresponded. And I was the first relative ever to meet her from Boise. And then she finally, her son came here first, and then she came here, and then after that, her family did. But I don't know how he came. When I was over there, when I went to visit, he wanted to take me to movies because he was working at Universal Studios and of course I was a young kid and I didn't ask why. What he was doing, I didn't care, you know. I could kick myself. You said that you would go to and get hamburgers with your boys when they were little. Were there hamburger places around? We used to go to That place on, what was it, no. It used to be on Broadway. It's not there anymore. It was called, we used to go get hamburgers. And I used to go get my uncle up at the VA and take him down there. And we'd all go have hamburgers there, too. We'd sit in the parking and eat the squirrels. Well, it sounds like the park played a big role for you and your family in a lot of different ways. Yeah, we used to come down here and eat all the time for seconds. Good place to be. Yeah. The most quiet, peaceful one, it was, you know, the trees were nice. Uh-huh. Well, do you have any questions? I'm sorry. I guess, since I didn't have the history, what did your parents do? Like what was the background? Meaning? What did your dad do? Your dad's part of the family. What was, how did they make their living? What was their environment like? I was working with a local person. OK. My grandfather came to America. And he started in Cleveland, Ohio. And he was a hardware and jewelry sales person. He had that kind of business. And they went through, they helped start Sun Valley, and sort of like Bailey, Bellevue, helped start Bellevue, too. And they came here, and they had a store downtown, a hardware store downtown. And then my dad was a dental supply salesman. What was the name of your grandfather's store? Do you remember? I don't know. I have pictures. There's no name. And then it was right next to the computer sauna building. And then my mother's side of the family, I hate to tell you this part. My grandfather was a cotton cat horse dealer. They say he didn't do it, but he spent a few years in the penitentiary out in the old penitentiary. Oh, in Boise? Mm-hmm. In Idaho. My grandmother worked down at St. Luke's forever in the kitchen. Oh really? What was her name? Edith Lester. And my mom worked for years for the schools being a cook and then she also worked for Sinker. And your mom's name? It was Lillian Seward. Or Lester. Yeah. You wrote it down already. Yeah. OK. And I worked for Albertsons for 23 years. You worked for Albertsons? I worked for Albertsons for 23 years, and then I got laid off in the fifth layoff. You just got laid off, you told me. Oh, in 2003, yeah. Then I said my current job. I just told her that. You did? Yeah. Well. We're alive. That's life, huh? Yeah. Well, Is there anything else that you think? I can think of something. I'll let you know. That's all I know. I think I've been thinking and thinking. Yeah. Well, it would be good for us to find out a little bit more about Gogo Fangle. OK. That's a fascinating story. It tells about how he got here and everything. It tells everything. I copied off of some of you. OK. Yeah, I can give you my copy. OK. OK. Good. Well, I appreciate this. Do you have anything else? Okay, thank you very much.

lines.line: # Introduction and Early Memories

> The interview begins with Tamalla Hart at the Idaho Historical Museum discussing her life in Boise. Born in 1958, she recounts learning to ice skate at Julia Davis Park Pond and being taught by Linda Lester. The family often held picnics at the park and enjoyed the band performances.
** introduction, Boise, Julia Davis Park, ice skating, picnics, band performances **
keywords: ** introduction, Boise, Julia Davis Park, ice skating, picnics, band performances **

metadata.title: Introduction and Early Memories
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with Tamalla Hart at the Idaho Historical Museum discussing her life in Boise. Born in 1958, she recounts learning to ice skate at Julia Davis Park Pond and being taught by Linda Lester. The family often held picnics at the park and enjoyed the band performances.
metadata.keywords: introduction, Boise, Julia Davis Park, ice skating, picnics, band performances

lines.line: # Family and Recreation in the Park

> Tamalla talks about family reunions with the Lesters and Sealips at the park, engaging in picnics, music, and skating. She also discusses attending the early cancer walks and Indian celebrations held in the park.
** family reunions, picnics, music, cancer walk, Indian celebration **
keywords: ** family reunions, picnics, music, cancer walk, Indian celebration **

metadata.title: Family and Recreation in the Park
metadata.synopsis: Tamalla talks about family reunions with the Lesters and Sealips at the park, engaging in picnics, music, and skating. She also discusses attending the early cancer walks and Indian celebrations held in the park.
metadata.keywords: family reunions, picnics, music, cancer walk, Indian celebration

lines.line: # Cousin's Visit from Germany

> A notable visit from Tamalla's cousin, Hanno Hoth, a famous film producer from Germany, is recounted. During his visit, the family took him to the local zoo, where Tamalla remembers scaring him at the snake exhibit.
** Germany, Hanno Hoth, film producer, zoo, snake exhibit **
keywords: ** Germany, Hanno Hoth, film producer, zoo, snake exhibit **

metadata.title: Cousin's Visit from Germany
metadata.synopsis: A notable visit from Tamalla's cousin, Hanno Hoth, a famous film producer from Germany, is recounted. During his visit, the family took him to the local zoo, where Tamalla remembers scaring him at the snake exhibit.
metadata.keywords: Germany, Hanno Hoth, film producer, zoo, snake exhibit

lines.line: # Park Activities and Attractions

> Recollections of childhood visits to the park's carnival rides such as the merry-go-round and Ferris wheel are shared. Tamalla also mentions participating in various park activities such as paddle boating and watching events with her children.
** carnival rides, merry-go-round, Ferris wheel, paddle boating, park events **
keywords: ** carnival rides, merry-go-round, Ferris wheel, paddle boating, park events **

lines.line: # Son's Deployment and Family Time
> Before Tamalla's son left for Iraq, the family spent time together in the park. She reflects on his military service and expresses gladness for the time they spent together there.
** son, Iraq, family time, military service **
keywords: ** son, Iraq, family time, military service **

story.title: Hart_Tamalla_Sielaff_OH 2702
story.description: Recorded on February 12, 2007. Topic is Julia Davis Park.
